title: What an Artifact Repository Is For
description:
## Where Do Build Outputs Live?

Your pipeline compiles code into `.jar` files and builds container images. What is the primary purpose of an **artifact repository** such as JFrog Artifactory, Sonatype Nexus, or a container registry?

options.text: A central, **versioned store of build outputs** (jars, packages, images) so deployments can pull a specific, immutable, reproducible version
A place to store your **source code** and its commit history, replacing Git
A system that **collects runtime metrics and logs** from production for dashboards
A tool that **provisions cloud servers** from declarative templates

explanation: An **artifact repository** stores the *outputs* of your build — compiled binaries, language packages, and container images — each tagged with a **version** so a deployment can fetch the exact, immutable artifact that was tested. This makes releases reproducible and rollbacks easy. It is distinct from **source control** (Git stores code, not built binaries — answer b), from **monitoring** stores that hold metrics/logs (c), and from **infrastructure provisioning** (d). The rule of thumb: source repos hold inputs, artifact repos hold versioned outputs.

hints.title: Inputs vs outputs
hints.body: Git versions the code you write; an artifact repository versions the binaries and images your pipeline produces from that code.
